items.q: What is the difference between random, stratified and systematic sampling?
items.correct: Random: every member has equal chance of selection. Systematic: pick every nth item. Stratified: divide the population into groups and sample proportionally from each — ensures subgroup representation.
items.misconception: All three sampling methods produce the same results.
items.random_wrong: Stratified sampling means picking samples from different strata of rock.
items.why: Stratified sampling is for ensuring subgroups (e.g. age groups, land-use types) are proportionately represented. The choice depends on the population structure — wrong choice gives biased results.

items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: How do hotspot volcanoes differ from subduction-zone volcanoes?
items.correct: Hotspots are stationary mantle plumes that punch through plates anywhere (e.g. Hawaii), while subduction-zone volcanoes form along plate boundaries from melting subducted crust.
items.misconception: Hotspot volcanoes form at plate boundaries like all other volcanoes.
items.random_wrong: Hotspot volcanoes are just very hot subduction volcanoes.
items.why: Hawaii sits in the middle of the Pacific Plate, nowhere near a boundary. The hotspot is fixed while the plate moves over it, producing volcanic chains — a different mechanism entirely.

items.q: What is the main driver of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: Correct answer: Cattle ranching. “Mining” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Emergent. “Canopy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of the Amazon has been lost since 1970?
items.why: Exact answer: 17 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
